summary: Gruber dismantles a Businessweek article advising Android users not to buy the new iPhones, arguing that switching platforms is easier today than ever thanks to cloud services and cheap apps. He particularly objects to the claim that platform-exclusive devices like Apple Watch exist primarily for lock-in purposes. Instead, he argues that deep hardware-software integration requires platform exclusivity — cross-platform smartwatches like Pebble demonstrate the functionality trade-offs. He notes the asymmetry of Google and Apple's rivalry, with Google actively developing quality iOS apps while Apple barely touches Android.
key_points: Cloud storage, syncing, and cheap/free apps make switching between iOS and Android easier than any prior platform transition in personal computing history
Apple Watch and Android Wear are platform-exclusive because deep integration requires coordinated hardware and software at the OS level, not because of competitive spite
Pebble's cross-platform approach demonstrates the severe functionality limitations of building for both iOS and Android simultaneously
Google and Apple's rivalry is asymmetric: Google actively develops premium iOS apps because iOS users are a lucrative advertising demographic
Arguing smartwatches should be cross-platform is like saying you should be able to put a Tesla engine in a Toyota Prius
money_quotes: Looking at the history of personal computing, I think it's easier to switch platforms today than ever before — in either direction.
Brustein's advice, to me, seems like an endorsement of laziness, ignorance, and tribalism.
Lock-in has nothing to do with why Apple Watch will only work with iPhone, or why Android Wear devices only work with Android phones.

Apple could make a watch that supports both iPhone and Android, but that watch wouldn't work anything like Apple Watch, because it would be severely limited by the common features shared by iPhone and Android.
Mobile apps can only provide shallow integration. To get deep integration requires software (and hardware) designed in coordination.
Brustein's argument is not too far removed from saying that we should be able to buy a Toyota Prius with a Tesla engine — like you can just mix and match these things like Lego bricks.
It's a pipe dream to think that Apple Watch and Android Wear could be cross-platform without a drastic reduction in functionality.
They don't treat iOS as a second-class platform — if anything, they're more interested in iOS users because they're a more lucrative demographic for advertisers.
key_insight: Platform-exclusive devices like Apple Watch exist not as lock-in strategies but because deep hardware-software integration is architecturally incompatible with cross-platform design.
